chunk_summary: Defines treatment failure, relapse, and post-recovery monitoring for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), emphasizing structured follow-up, operational audits, and graduated monitoring schedules to prevent recurrence and ensure recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for early failure and non-response in MAM treatment.
Learn operational definitions of relapse and thresholds for referral in MAM management.
Explore post-recovery monitoring strategies to prevent recurrence of malnutrition.
key_facts: Early failure is defined as clinical deterioration or anthropometric worsening within 1–2 weeks of treatment initiation.
Non-response is identified after 2 structured follow-up visits (2–4 weeks) with no improvement in appetite, feeding behavior, or anthropometric trajectory.
Persistent non-response by 8 weeks is a practical threshold for referral to higher facilities in Indian protocols.
Relapse is defined as re-eligibility for MAM criteria within 2 months of discharge, including MUAC 115–124 mm or WHZ/WLZ -3 to -2.
Post-recovery monitoring should align with service touchpoints and include graduated schedules.
reference_quote: Relapse is clinically meaningful because it signals unresolved household food insecurity, recurrent infection burden, or unaddressed comorbidity. Indian facility-based malnutrition guidance defines relapse operationally as re-eligibility within about 2 months after cure.
